title: Post by David Diaz
description:
Ray is shutting down. We ended operations this week, and I'd rather share it honestly than let it trickle out.
...
Ray started as a browser built for games. Our running joke was that Chrome was for Boomers and Ray was for Play. Somewhere along the way we saw a bigger problem: the browser sits on top of everything you do online, and none of that context was actually working for you. So we pivoted, and spent the last stretch building Ray into a browser that used your own context to help you.
...
What still gets me is what a team of ten pulled off. We shipped a browser 30% faster than Chrome. We took our compile times from over 24 hours down to under one. We built what I still think is the best split screen anyone's done in a browser — I'm going to miss it when Ray stops working. Ten people did all of that. And more than 500,000 people downloaded Ray and played tens of millions of hours on it.
...
In the end we couldn't raise what we needed or find a home for it, and we ran out of road.
.

title: Loom's Post-Acquisition Decline Under Atlassian | Alberto Gimeno posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: Getting acquired by Atlassian should come with a warning label. Loom was fast, clean, genuinely delightful. Now users are calling it the fastest post-acquisition decline they've seen. Rivaling what Salesforce did to Slack. Here's what always kills these products: the roadmap stops serving users and starts serving the parent's cross-sell goals. Every feature decision filters through "how does this drive Jira adoption?" instead of "how does this make recordings better?" The product doesn't get worse by accident. It gets worse on purpose. These companies acquire products because they can't build anything that good. Then they prove exactly why. Every botched acquisition reopens a market. Someone's already rebuilding what Loom used to be.\n...\nGetting acquired by Atlassian should come with a warning label. Loom was fast, clean, genuinely delightful. Here’s something cool that [Atlassian](https://linkedin.com/company/atlassian) and [Dropbox](https://linkedin.com/company/dropbox) have in common with our AI transformation journeys.

During the pandemic, both companies made deliberate decisions to set themselves up to work incredibly effectively while remote. That meant two things:
✔️ We had to drive a people-led, enterprise-wide transformation
✔️ We built a culture of async work: everything documented, communication mostly written (or Loom) and teams were open by default

It wasn’t designed to set us up for AI transformation, but that’s exactly what it’s done.

title: OpenAI Kills ChatGPT Atlas: Why the Browser Was Never the Real Battlefield - FourWeekMBA
description:
MacRumors reported Thursday that OpenAI has confirmed ChatGPT Atlas — its standalone AI browser for Mac, launched in October 2025 — will be shut down on August 9, 2026. That’s fewer than ten months after launch, which by any measure is a short run for a product OpenAI billed as a new interface paradigm: a browser that could carry out tasks on your behalf, not just surface information.
...
But read the fine print before calling this a retreat. OpenAI is not abandoning the ambition. The agentic browsing capabilities are being folded into a substantially upgraded ChatGPT desktop app — multiple tabs, password management, autofill, downloads, page search, passkeys, enterprise sign-in, ChatGPT Work, and Codex integration — and a new Chrome extension that adds highlighted-text actions, tab controls, access to local files, and persistent task management. Atlas users are being notified with transition details ahead of the deadline.
...
The Atlas shutdown is the most instructive counter-example

title: 不到一年半，首个iOS第三方应用商店就凉了-36氪
description:
但两年过去，iOS生态的分裂不仅没有发生，《数字市场法案》催生的一系列第三方iOS应用商店反而撑不下去了。日前软件开发商MacPaw发布公告称，将于2026年2月16日关闭旗下iOS第三方应用商店Setapp Mobile，在这一截止日期后，用户通过Setapp Mobile安装的所有iOS应用将从设备中被移除、且无法使用。
...
此前在2024年春季，当苹果宣布为遵守《数字市场法》的规定，将通过iOS 17.4在欧盟实施多项变革，其中就包括第三方应用商店。消息一出MacPaw旋即响应，Setapp Mobile也成为了首个第三方iOS应用商店。
...
那么问题就来了，天时地利人和俱在的情况下，Setapp Mobile为何仅仅存续了不到一年半时间呢？答案其实是苹果棋高一着，用“核心技术费”（Core Technology Fee）掐住了第三方应用商店的咽喉。
...
MacPaw在支持页面中直言不讳地指出，第三方应用商店面临“仍在演变且复杂的商业条款”，是Setapp Mobile关闭的直接原因。按照苹果方面的规定，无论App Store、还是第三方应用商店分发的iOS应用，一款App每年安装量超过100万次后，开发者就需要每年为用户的第一次安装支付0.5欧元。
...
没错，苹果的核心技术使用费与游戏引擎Unity当初搞的按次收费一模一样，抬高了开发者使用第三方应用商店的成本，导致App越畅销反而越赔钱。如果App有内购服务，还需要使用苹果官方支付渠道（IAP），虽然此时苹果确实不会收取30%的抽成，但开发者也需要至少缴纳17%的费用。
...
这一套组合拳下来，知名手游登陆Setapp Mobile反而可能支付比App Store更多的费用，而不知名的App上Setapp Mobile，又很容易被埋没。没了“苹果税”、却多出“核心技术费”，而且还蹭不到App Store的影响力，如此一来，无论哪种体量的App上Setapp Mobile都没什么性价比。
...
Setapp Mobile最大的挑战就是用户的信任成本，导致它被苹果的“核心技术费”拖垮。反观“外部支付”，就既让开发者享受到了App Store的便利，又跳过了使用App Store要付出的代价，属于典型的“搭便车”，这也是为什么苹果在美国和日本为了取消外部支付而进行积极游说。

title: Meta AI Agents Behind Schedule: Zuckerberg Tells Staff $145B Bet Hasn't Delivered
description:
Zuckerberg told employees that the "trajectory of the agentic development over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected," and that the company's bets on its newly restructured organization "haven't come to fruition yet." He acknowledged the reorganization was not as "clean" as it could have been, and that executives had "miscalculated on the timing" of the changes.

Meta laid off about 8,000 employees — roughly 10 percent of its global workforce of approximately 78,000 — in May, and simultaneously transferred approximately 7,000 employees into newly formed AI-focused teams, including the Applied AI Engineering unit and the Agent Transformation Accelerator. Together, those changes touched close to one-fifth of the company's total headcount.

title: Atlassian Dropped New AI Dev Tools — Let’s Talk About It – The Jira Guy
description: Governance becomes a first-class deliverable. I cannot stress this point enough. “Autonomy has to stay observable” is the line, and the session-visibility features are how they deliver on it. But observability you don’t configure or review is just logging nobody reads. Somebody has to decide who’s allowed to assign work to agents, which projects get the native coding agent turned loose, how session records are audited, and what the review gate looks like before an agent’s PR can merge. That “somebody” is going to be us. This is the part of the job that looks less like classic Jira admin and more like platform governance — and it’s the part I’d start skilling up on today.\n...\nThe through-line: the work is shifting from configuring a system for people to governing a system where people and agents work side by side. That’s not a small adjustment. It’s arguably the biggest change to what an Atlassian admin does since the Cloud migration— and unlike that one, there’s no multi-year runway. I

description:

# OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, but its AI browser ambitions are still growing
...
OpenAI is sunsetting Atlas, the AI-powered browser it launched in October with ChatGPT at its core. But it’s not giving up on the idea that AI should help people browse the web. Instead, it’s taking some of the agentic browsing features it tested in Atlas and redistributing them across ChatGPT’s desktop app and a Google Chrome extension.
...
The move to shut down Atlas comes a few months after OpenAI’s former CEO of applications Fidji Simo told the team to cut back on “side quests,” which led to the AI firm shutting down its AI video-generation tool Sora.
...

title: Dropbox Analyst Downgrade Due to AI Stagnation

description: Atlassian conducted layoffs in March 2026 specifically linked to AI disruption and the failure of enterprise generative AI projects to deliver value, mirroring the high failure rates reported by MIT in the sector.

description: Dropbox CEO Drew Houston stepped down in May 2026 to facilitate a strategic pivot. 🇺🇦🛡️ Ukrainian defence tech funding map 2026-2027

Ukrainian defence tech has grown more than twentyfold in funding over two years. But working out who gives money and whether Ukrainians can apply directly has become a task in itself. So we split donors into three tiers.

🇺🇦 Tier 1: national
🔹 Brave1: up to UAH 8M, up to UAH 30M/year, direct application
🔹 EU4UA Defence Tech: up to €150K, EU co-funded
🔹 Brave International: over €100M, joint 50/50 funds with partner countries

🛡️ Tier 2: international defence
🔹 EDF: €1B for 2026, Ukraine via partner for now
🔹 EUDIS: €231M, Ukrainian companies eligible for cascade grants directly
🔹 NATO DIANA: up to €100K in Phase 1, but only via a legal entity in a NATO country

🔹 Horizon Europe: direct application, Ukraine is an associated member

title: Ukraine Bans Provider-Controlled AI Systems | Model Diplomat
description:
Ukraine will procure only AI systems it can host on its own infrastructure for government, business and military use, Roman Kyslyi, Chief AI Officer at Kyiv's Ministry of Digital Transformation, told Reuters on July 7. The policy — the first from any wartime European government — effectively locks Anthropic's Claude and OpenAI's flagship models out of Ukrainian state contracts, and reframes AI procurement as a sovereignty question rather than a performance one. title: We can't stop candidates using AI, so let's accept it as the new normal
description: In court, the ground is even weaker, at least in Europe. Recruitment AI is high-risk under the EU AI Act, and reading emotions or monitoring behaviour on camera, which is what most detection tools do, sits in banned or restricted territory. Much of the tooling built for the American market cannot lawfully run in Europe.\n...\nStage 3 and 4: Shortlist, then have them walk you through it. A quick human review narrows the 50 to around 15 for a live conversation, fifteen minutes, and ideally an unscheduled phone call rather than a booked video slot, because an unscheduled call is far harder to feed through an AI assistant in real time. One request carries it: walk me through your work and explain your choices. Someone who outsourced their thinking struggles within minutes, no detection software needed. ...

This means visibility into what’s running, policy controls enforced directly on the endpoint, and reporting that helps security teams demonstrate compliance. Only 35% of CIOs have full visibility into their AI operating costs, according to a new KPMG survey. That makes it difficult for them to control spend on software-as-a-service offerings from vendors who, like Workday, have incorporated pay-as-you-go agentic AI into their offerings. Workday is one of several vendors that have shifted to a hybrid subscription/consumption pricing model.
...
The changes Monroy described are part of an industry trend, according to Terra Higginson, principal research director at Info-Tech Research Group. “What we are seeing in the market is that basic seat pricing and seat counts are not going away. Customers are still paying for the core subscription footprint. AI is being layered on top as an incremental cost,” she said. “The practical message is simple: expect to pay more. The pricing model may shift from seats to credits or consumption, but the direction of spend is still up.”

Fusion Agentic Applications represent a new class of enterprise application: outcome-driven systems backed by teams of specialized AI agents that reason, coordinate, and decide, then execute work through Fusion business objects, workflows, tools, policies, approvals, and logged actions.

They run natively in Oracle Fusion Applications, inherit Fusion security and governance controls, act against Fusion business objects and workflows, and provide the auditability required for enterprise execution.

title: Building Enterprise AI Agents That Are Both Autonomous and Reliable
description:
The hard part of enterprise AI is not making models smarter. It is resolving a real tension between two forces that pull in opposite directions: the agency that makes an LLM useful, and the control that makes a workflow trustworthy. Over the past year, building the runtime behind Agentforce, we have organized our entire architecture around reconciling those two forces. We call the approach guided determinism. It rests on Agent Graph orchestration, the Agent Script language, specialized subagents, and fine-tuned routing models, all running across globally distributed Hyperforce infrastructure. This is what we learned building it.

layer is application: platforms that let a single company, a hospital, a bank, run AI inside its own infrastructure without touching the first two layers at all. This is the layer where sovereignty is genuinely achievable for a normal company, because you are not trying to out-build Nvidia or train a frontier model. You are just keeping your own data and inference inside your own walls. This is also the layer worth looking at closely, because it is the one most businesses will actually interact with.

The second is trust. Normally, building an AI model means sending your data to a vendor, who trains on it and holds onto it. For hospitals, banks, and governments, that is a real risk. Sensitive data leaving the building means regulatory exposure and a loss of control. title: Introducing Claude apps gateway for AWS | Artificial Intelligence
description:
Enterprises deploying Claude Code and Claude Desktop across development teams need centralized control over access, cost, and policy. At scale, this is hard to manage: each developer needs an individual credential, settings must be distributed manually, and spend is difficult to track or cap. Without a centralized control point, governance is left to whatever tooling each team can implement independently.
...
Today, we’re announcing the Claude apps gateway for AWS, a self-hosted control plane that gives organizations a single point of control over access, cost, and policy for Claude Code and Claude Desktop. It replaces the need to provision a separate cloud credential per developer, push settings to every laptop by hand, or stand up separate tooling to track spend. You can deploy it through Amazon Bedrock to keep data within the AWS security boundary, or through Claude Platform on AWS to get the same gateway controls with the native Claude platform experience.

title: Claude Merges Chat and Cowork into Single Home Tab | JJ Englert posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: Finally. Claude just merged Chat and Cowork into a single Home tab, and this is the one I've been wanting since Cowork launched. Before this, you had to pick a lane before you even opened a conversation: Chat if you just wanted to talk, Cowork if you needed Claude to read your files and get to work. Now it's one tab, and you just start typing. That's not even the best part of this update. You can now turn any conversation into a Skill, or schedule it to run on its own, every Monday at 9am, no touching it again. If you're on a Team account, it goes further: - Projects are now shared org-wide now. Including Cowork projects, which used to be stuck on one person's laptop. Client files, proposals, timelines, estimates, all in one shared workspace. Skills, connectors, and plugins share across the org too. Artifacts got real sharing. title: 1Password and Anthropic Bring Secure Credential Access to Claude, letting an AI agent use approved credentials without e
description: Until now, giving an AI agent access to credentials in a browser meant passing them as plain text into the agent's context, where they were directly accessible to the model. 1Password built a zero-exposure security framework that allows agents to use stored credentials in the 1Password vault without them ever reaching the model. Rather than forcing users to choose between sharing their credentials or manually authenticating, it gives agents exactly what they need, scoped to the task, with multiple layers of protection that ensure secrets remain encrypted until the moment they are injected into the page by 1Password. Access is granted per session and scoped to a specific set of approved items. That authorization does not carry over to other sessions, eliminating standing access. 1Password for Claude is the first integration built on it. MCP in 2 minutes (and 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 should you use it): Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard way for an AI agent or application to connect to outside tools and data. You can connect to more than one MCP server at a time. The server is what actually talks to outside applications and data. Two things MCP is not: ● It is not "tool use." Tool use is the agent's ability to call a function; MCP is the standard for how some functions get described and discovered in
...
● It is not a replacement for APIs. An MCP server almost always sits on top of an existing API and calls it for you. A server can expose three kinds of capability: ● 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 are actions the agent runs on its own while it works, like querying a database or opening a pull request. ● 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲𝘀 are reference data the app loads in for context, like a file, a schema, or a document. The agent reads them; it does not go fetch them

title: Microsoft and MCP: How the Ecosystem Is Embracing the Model Context Protocol
description: The Model Context Protocol, commonly known as MCP, is an open standard originally introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. Its core purpose is to give AI models a standardized, vendor-neutral way to connect with external tools, data sources, APIs, and services. Before MCP, integrating an AI assistant with a business system required bespoke connectors — a fragmented, expensive, and time-consuming effort for every new tool or model combination. MCP resolves this by functioning essentially like a universal plugin system for AI: any application that exposes an MCP server can be accessed by any AI agent or assistant that supports the MCP client standard. It defines how AI systems can discover available tools, request their execution, and consume the results in a structured, secure, and interoperable way.\n...\nMicrosoft's endorsement of MCP is not peripheral — it reflects a deliberate strategy to make interoperability the foundation of its AI platform ambitions. In early 2025, Microsoft form

# Post by Piyush Gupta · LinkedIn · 2026-07-10

**Piyush Gupta**: Implementation Body Member at PEC ACM CSS with 2 years 9 months of experience. Previous roles include Member at PEC ACM CSS. Based in Pehowa, Haryana, India [IN].

Every AI has a memory. None of them share it.

So I built one that does.

The demo video shows the same conversation flowing between Gemini, ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude and my terminal. Switching tools feels like continuing the conversation instead of restarting it.

That's what Recall does.

How?
A single SQLite database becomes the shared brain.
1. Every MCP compatible tool reads from and writes to it.
2. A lightweight browser extension connects ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
3. Everything is local first.
4. Nothing leaves your machine unless you want it to.

The project is fully open source.

title: Is There an MCP Server for AI Agent Memory? - DEV Community
description:
Yes. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an open protocol published by Anthropic in November 2024, specification version 2025-11-25 — standardizes how LLM applications connect to external tools and data sources, and several MCP servers exist specifically for agent memory. The official MCP servers repository includes a knowledge graph-based memory server (`@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory`), and third-party memory servers — including PLUR, Zep, Mem0's OpenMemory, and community projects — expose persistent agent memory through the same protocol. The practical question is not whether an MCP memory server exists, but which one fits your needs, because "MCP-compatible" tells you the transport, not the memory format.

title: Medium
description: This is one of the fastest-moving corners of agentic AI in 2026, and it’s converging on one clean architectural answer: expose memory as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. Instead of baking memory logic into one specific app, you build it once as a standalone server with a handful of tools —`remember`,`recall`,`forget`— and any MCP-compatible agent (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, a custom client you write yourself) can plug into it immediately, over the same open protocol Anthropic ships with Claude itself.\n...\nThe reason this matters for topic selection right now: the Model Context Protocol, introduced by Anthropic in late 2024 as an open standard for connecting AI systems to external tools and data sources, has become the connective tissue for exactly this kind of capability. title: Memory Belongs to the User: Portable Memory, an Open Standard Proposal for Cross-Vendor AI Memory · MacPaw Research
description: Memory Belongs to the User: Portable Memory, an Open Standard Proposal for Cross-Vendor AI Memory · MacPaw Research\n\nAI assistants became systems that remember: preferences, projects, relationships, and history now persist across sessions and increasingly shape what these systems do for us. That memory is the most personal dataset a person delegates to software — and today it is locked inside each vendor's proprietary storage, in incompatible shapes, with no way to move it and no way to prove it has ever been deleted. We believe this is the data portability gap of the AI era, and that it should be closed the way the web closed it for documents, mail, and calendars: with an open format. This paper proposes Portable Memory, an open, vendor-neutral standard for carrying AI memory across applications, devices, and vendors — lossless, local, and with verifiable deletion as its guarantee of integrity. We present the vision and principles; an overview of the format (a self-contained`.mem` bun

# Post by OJOBIT · LinkedIn · 2026-07-05

Espresso decodes 1.08 ms per token on an M3 Max, 4.76x faster than Apple's CoreML.cpuAndNeuralEngine path at 5.09 ms.

→ Espresso is a Swift library that compiles MIL programs directly to the Apple Neural Engine via reverse-engineered private APIs (ANEClient, ANEInMemoryModel) and moves data through IOSurface buffers with zero marshaling.
→ Fused triplet kernels pack 3 transformer layers into a single ANE dispatch, reducing 6 layers to 2 eval calls. KV cache lives entirely in IOSurface buffers, untouched by the CPU during decode.
→ The benchmark runs a 6-layer transformer with hidden dim 768, 12 heads, 32k vocabulary, sequence length 256 on macOS 15. Results are reproducible with the project's scripts.
→ Against llama.cp

title: Ollama Raises $65M for Easier Open AI Model Deployment | Y Combinator posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: Ollama has raised $65M in Series B funding to make open AI models easier for developers to run, customize, and deploy. Open models have become dramatically more capable over the past few years, but they've often been difficult for developers to use. Getting models running has typically required configuring hardware, inference engines, and other tooling before developers could start building applications. Founded by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang, the team behind Kitematic and Docker Desktop, Ollama lets developers download and run open models with a single command. The platform works across local machines and the cloud, giving developers a simple way to build with models while maintaining ownership of their models and data. Today, Ollama serves 8.9 million developers and is used by 85% of the Fortune 500. All with just 14 employees. Their cloud platform has more than doubled in token volume every month as more teams adopt open models for production workloads. The new funding will he

title: Ollama v0.32.1 Released — a Release Candidate Patches the MLX Memory Leak From v0.32.0 | AppSelfHost
description:
Ollama, the self-hosted runtime that lets anyone run large language models on their own hardware without touching a cloud API, has shipped v0.32.1 — just five days after v0.32.0. For a project with 176,000+ GitHub stars and a release cadence that rarely slows down, a five-day turnaround usually means one thing: something in the previous release needed fixing quickly.
...
That’s exactly the case here. The changelog is tagged against`v0.32.1-rc0` in the diff and the release itself is flagged as a pre-release on GitHub, so this is best understood as a release-candidate patch rather than a routine feature drop. The headline fix addresses a recurring memory leak in the MLX model cache — relevant to anyone running Ollama on Apple Silicon, where MLX is the backend of choice.
...
The rest of the update is smaller in scope: continued polish for Gemma 4’s tool-calling behavior, a timeout fix for MLX text models, and some quality-of-life improvements to the interactive agent and web search flows.

description:
Ollama has become the default on-ramp for self-hosters who want to run large language models on their own hardware, with a simple CLI, a REST API, and a model library that covers everything from Llama and Qwen to MLX-optimized builds for Apple Silicon. Its 175,000+ GitHub stars reflect how central it’s become to the local-AI stack, sitting underneath everything from Open WebUI installs to home-lab coding assistants.
...
v0.31.2-rc2 is a release candidate, and it’s a more consequential one than the version bump suggests. The headline item is the first landing of an internal "agent harness core" — early scaffolding for native agentic tool-use inside Ollama itself, rather than leaving that entirely to downstream clients. Alongside it, there’s a meaningful GPU compatibility shakeup: Flash Attention is now enabled on older CUDA compute-capability 6.x cards (think GTX 10-series Pascal GPUs), while ROCm support quietly drops several AMD devices that are no longer maintained upstream.

title: Ollama vs LM Studio: Picking a Local LLM Runtime in 2026 · Groundy
description:
For three years the correct answer to “Ollama or LM Studio?” was a shrug. Both wrapped llama.cpp, the reasoning went, so they generated tokens at the same speed and the only real difference was a command line versus a graphical app11. That was true, and then it stopped being true. In the year leading into mid-2026, LM Studio dropped its commercial-license requirement, Ollama built an MLX backend into its Apple Silicon path, and the two tools converged on the same feature surface they used to differentiate on. What is left to decide is not which inference engine you get. It is the developer surface, the license, and a ceiling neither tool advertises.
...
Until July 2
...
LM Studio is a desktop application first and a server second. Its strength is the part Ollama deliberately does not build: a graphical interface for searching Hugging Face, downloading GGUF or MLX checkpoints, loading several models at once, and chatting with them without writing a line of configuration. title: Medium
description:
During WWDC week — June 8 to 12, 2026 — Apple shipped a Swift class called`MLXLanguageModel` that barely made the keynote highlight reel. Point it at almost any of the roughly 4,800 models sitting inside Hugging Face's mlx-community organization, and your app talks to that model through the exact same Foundation Models API Apple uses for its own on-device system model. Tool calling, structured output, streaming — identical code path, different brain underneath.
...
Here’s the contrarian part: the format war for on-device Mac inference is already over, and nobody won it with a benchmark. A Hugging Face Space called mlx-my-repo won it by making the entry cost zero. No Python. No terminal. No local setup. You paste a model ID, you wait, you get a working MLX repo. That’s the whole workflow, and almost nobody outside the MLX community is writing about it.
...
mlx-my-repo isn’t a separate conversion engine. It’s a web front end wired to`mlx-lm`, the Python package the MLX team maintains for

title: MLX vs llama.cpp on Mac: 40% Faster, But There's a Catch | CraftRigs
description:
MLX is Apple's native machine-learning array framework, released December 2023 and developed by its machine-learning research group, designed to exploit the unified memory architecture of M-series chips where CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine share a single memory pool. Apple built it as a direct answer to PyTorch and TensorFlow treating Mac as an afterthought, forcing developers through layers of abstraction that ignored the hardware's most distinctive advantage. The framework arrived with immediate Metal Performance Shaders integration. Every operation compiles directly to Apple's GPU instruction set. Cross-platform code needs translation layers that slow it down. For local LLM users, inference is a memory-bandwidth problem. You're moving billions of quantized weights through the memory subsystem as fast as possible. Any inefficiency in that path directly subtracts from your tok/s.
...

---

Mac malware is evolving rapidly, and PamStealer is a prime example of how attackers leverage native macOS features against users—especially on Apple Silicon devices. This malware masquerades as the legitimate clipboard tool Maccy, targeting users who download it from fake sites. Using AppleScript, it restricts execution to Apple Silicon Macs while excluding Eastern European systems, showcasing targeted, adaptive behavior.

What really stands out is how PamStealer interacts with the macOS PAM framework to repeatedly request the user’s login password, validating credentials locally before stealing sensitive data including browser passwords, crypto wallets, and iCloud Keychain entries. title: Apple has released an update to XProtect for macOS - MacDailyNews
description:
Apple has released an update to XProtect for macOS - MacDailyNews

Apple has released a new update to XProtect, its built-in malware protection tool, for all macOS versions.

This update bumps XProtect to version 5351 (from 5347) — the first broad release since early June — and adds fresh detection rules for several threats, including the Boatload stealer family and new osascript-based malware.

As usual, Apple provides no public details on the specific threats addressed. All users should check that the update has installed.

The Eclectic Light Company: ‎

This version adds six new Yara rules for MACOS.ANGRYORB.OX, MACOS.BOATLOAD.STCRST, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PRIN, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PEME, MACOS.VSHELL.DITA and MACOS.VSHELL.DR, amends rules for MACOS.MISOMAGIC.PA, and tidies spacing for those for MACOS.SOMA.STTX. From the rules for Boatload, that would appear to be a stealer.

Osascript rules in XPScripts.yr gain no less than 14 new rules, and amend those for MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TIPA, MACOS.OSASCRIPT

title: CrashStealer: C++ macOS Infostealer Posing as Crash Reporter
description:
Unlike much of the commodity stealer activity on macOS, which is built on AppleScript droppers or thin Objective-C wrappers, CrashStealer is implemented in native C++ around an internal class the authors named`MacOSData`. It validates the victim's login password locally before harvesting, collects broadly across browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers and the keychain, encrypts what it collects with AES-GCM before exfiltrating over libcurl, and persists by copying and re-signing itself. Although its objectives overlap with families such as Atomic (AMOS), MacSync and Phexia, its native C++ implementation and client-side encryption set it apart, and we track it as a distinct family rather than a variant.
...

title: MCP 2.0: The Biggest Update Yet to the Protocol Connecting AI to Your Tools - By Fredy Cerna
description:
The business value is here: instead of maintaining a separate integration for every tool and every model, you expose your capabilities once through MCP. An internal app, a third-party system, or an API becomes available to any compatible AI assistant under a single contract — less custom code, less dependence on a single vendor, and an integration surface that is easier to govern.
...
go away: the initialization handshake and the
...
header in the Streamable HTTP transport are removed. (This change, like
...
others below, comes from a Specification Enhancement Proposal — an SEP, the community’s formal mechanism for proposing changes to the protocol, similar to Python’s PEPs; this one is SEP-2575.) In practice, a remote server that used to need sticky sessions and a shared session store can now run behind a common load balancer. State stops hiding in the transport and becomes explicit: the server returns an identifier (a handle) and the client passes it back on subsequent

title: SNI: Week 28
description: On Smooth Scaling, Platform94's Marie Donnellan interviewed Brian Hanly – our very own CEO. His line for anyone pricing a crash: there may be a valuation bubble, but there is no utility bubble, because 'if OpenAI went out of business in the morning, it doesn't matter' – the capability is already commoditised. The real constraint sits with us: 'the biggest obstacle isn't technology, it's the rate at which we can all absorb change'. title: WARNING: New macOS Malware Impersonates Apple's Notarized Installer
description:
A newly identified macOS information-stealing malware family, dubbed CrashStealer, is raising concerns after leveraging an Apple-notarized installer to bypass one of macOS's most important security protections. By abusing Apple's trusted software signing and notarization process, the malware is able to execute without triggering the Gatekeeper security warnings that users typically rely on to identify potentially malicious applications.
...
One of the most notable aspects of the campaign is its use of a signed and Apple-notarized installer known as Werkbit Setup. Apple's notarization service is designed to scan software for known malicious content before allowing it to run without warning on macOS systems. Applications that successfully pass the notarization process are generally trusted by both the operating system and end users.
...
By obtaining notarization for its installer, the operators behind CrashStealer effectively allow the malware to bypass Gatekeeper, the built-in macOS sec

title: Apple Intelligence Isn’t as Private as You Think. Here’s What Actually Leaves Your Mac. | by Anup Karanjkar | Mac O’Cloc
description:
When the local model cannot cope, Apple sends the request to Private Cloud Compute. This is not a normal cloud AI. Apple built it around five hard requirements: stateless computation, enforceable guarantees, no privileged runtime access, non-targetability, and verifiable transparency.
...
In plain terms, per Apple’s own Security Research documentation and independent breakdowns of it: your request is used only to answer you and is then deleted, with nothing written to disk and nothing kept in logs. Administrators cannot reach into a node and read your data, even during an outage. Your device refuses to talk to a server unless that server can cryptographically prove it is running the exact software Apple published. And Apple releases the PCC source code, in the`apple/security-pcc` project on GitHub, so outside researchers can check that the deployed system matches the promise.

title: Apple App Privacy Labels Required September 2026: Act Now
description:
Here is what happened. Apple updated its App Store submission requirements. Starting September 2026, you cannot submit a new app or push an update without completing a revised set of privacy disclosure questions. Not partially completing them. Not skipping the sections that seem complicated. All of them, fully answered, before the submission goes through.

The first is that the questionnaire got longer. Apple built the original version before AI features became standard in consumer apps. The questions did not account for apps that send user conversations to a language model, use personal context to generate recommendations, or integrate third-party AI services that have their own data handling practices. All of that is covered now, and if your app touches any of it, there are specific new questions you have to answer.

title: DOJ and FTC Shift Antitrust Landscape for Healthcare, Energy, Housing, and Agriculture Deals | A&O Shearman U.S. and Ame
description:
The antitrust landscape has shifted for anyone planning a deal in healthcare, energy, housing, or agriculture. The Department of Justice (DOJ) and Federal Trade Commission (FTC) are prioritizing mergers and acquisitions that affect what every-day Americans pay. Healthcare alone accounts for 60% of merger challenges under the current administration, and the FTC's new Healthcare Task Force shows this focus will remain. Four things dealmakers in consumer-facing industries should be thinking about in this administration: • Heightened scrutiny applies regardless of deal size. • Healthcare deals are likely to face more coordinated review by various FTC bureaus. • Agencies will entertain robust divestiture packages that address competitive concerns, but early planning will be important. • Agencies will still litigate when they need to. Don't assume a settlement is guaranteed. The playbook has changed. Read more here: https://lnkd.in/e9hSPdfE

title: Post by Despoina (Deni) Mantzari
description:
Very interesting to see the CMA's new Steering Conduct Requirement consultation on Apple's App Store (published 30 June 2026) converge on a fee framework that closely echoes the principles I set out in "Regulating FRAND Access to App Stores under the DMA" (JCLE, 2026).
...
My article argued that FRAND platform access under DMA Article 6(12) needs four things:
...
1. gatekeepers shouldn't be paid for network-effect rents;
2. fees must reflect the reciprocal value developers create for the ecosystem;
3. pricing should account for the net value exchange between platform and developer, not just one direction; and,
5. costs should be assessed on a long-run incremental basis.
...

Currently, app developers in the UK are generally not allowed to sell digital goods and services to UK device users other than through Apple’s In-App Payment (IAP) system, at a headline commission rate of 30%. Nor are they allowed to ‘steer’ end-users to offers for digital goods and services provided elsewhere (e.g., via their website) – a restriction that firms such as Spotify have fallen foul of in recent years. However, similar to interventions elsewhere in the US, EU, Japan, and Brazil, the proposed Steering CR would remove such restrictions for all apps made available to end-users in the UK. ‘Strictly necessary’ exceptions are made for user trust, security, and privacy factors, but it is notable that these are generally defined narrowly by the CMA. # Post by Marco Scialdone · LinkedIn · 2026-07-08

**Marco Scialdone**: Head Litigation & Academic Outreach at Euroconsumers with 5 years 3 months of experience. Based in Italy [IT].

---

The freshly delivered judgment of the EU General Court in the Apple case marks a watershed moment in the early enforcement history of the Digital Markets Act.

Apple sought nothing less than a declaration under Article 277 TFEU to render Article 6(7) of the DMA entirely inapplicable. By leveraging the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, specifically the principle of proportionality under Article 52(1) and the right to property, Apple launched a direct assault on the statutory obligation to provide free-of-charge, effective interoperability and access to hardware and software features controlled by its operating system.

Had this plea succeeded, it would have structurally disarmed the DMA, removing one of its most potent tools designed to dismantle walled gardens.

title: Apple Loses EU Court Battle Over App Store and iPhone DMA Rules
description:
Apple has lost a landmark court battle in Europe, with the EU’s General Court in Luxembourg dismissing the company’s challenge against its designation as a “gatekeeper” under the bloc’s Digital Markets Act. The ruling means Apple’s App Store and iOS operating system remain fully subject to the DMA’s sweeping competition requirements — a defeat with potentially enormous financial and operational consequences for the world’s most valuable company.
...
Judges at the EU General Court ruled on July 8, 2026, that European regulators were correct to designate Apple’s App Store and iPhone operating system as “gatekeeper” platforms under the Digital Markets Act. The DMA, which came into force in May 2023, imposes strict obligations on dominant technology platforms — including requirements to allow third-party app stores, interoperability with rival services, and restrictions on self-preferencing practices that disadvantage competitors.

title: Apple Loses Major 'Gatekeeper' EU Antitrust Appeal
description:
The European General Court has rejected Apple’s antitrust appeals against its “gatekeeper” classification under the region’s Digital Markets Act. The ruling upholds regulations requiring the tech giant to permit rival software interoperability within iOS and across its five digital App Stores. While Apple argued that the mandate compromises user privacy and security protections, European judges sided with regulators aiming to foster market competition.
...
The legal battle stems from the EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA), a legislation designed to prevent tech monopolies from suffocating smaller competitors. Under these rules, regulators slapped Apple with a “gatekeeper” designation. This legally forced the iPhone maker to open up its software architecture and ensure competing hardware can easily communicate with its operating systems. Apple filed multiple lawsuits to strip away these labels, but European judges systematically dismantled every single argument the company presented.

title: Apple’s DMA Defeat Is a Win for Platform Choice – and Microsoft Could Be Next - Windows News
description: Apple’s two-year legal fight to dodge the European Union’s Digital Markets Act crashed to a halt on Wednesday morning in Luxembourg. The General Court tossed out every appeal Apple had lodged, confirming that iOS, the App Store, and Safari all qualify as gatekeeper services under the DMA. The ruling doesn’t just force Apple to keep its platform open – it sends a clear signal that the law’s designations are built to withstand corporate pushback, with direct knock-on effects for Microsoft and Windows users.\n...\nThe court wasn’t persuaded. In a comprehensive ruling handed down March 19, judges backed the Commission on every count. They confirmed that even with the tight integration Apple pointed to, iOS and the App Store are distinct services that each serve as an important gateway between businesses and consumers. title: EU Court dismisses Apple appeal: the company is a ‘gatekeeper’

Furthermore, the Commission had classified the iMessage service as a number-independent interpersonal communications service (NIICS) constituting a core platform service (CPS). In this context, Apple had brought an appeal before the General Court of the European Union to challenge “its designation as a gatekeeper for the App Store and iOS, as well as certain classifications adopted by the Commission” and the decision concerning iMessage.\n...\nToday, the Court dismissed all appeals, confirming Apple’s designation as a gatekeeper for the App Store and iOS, and declared the appeals relating to the iMessage service inadmissible. The obligations that developers care about — allowing alternative app marketplaces and web distribution on iOS in the EU, letting developers steer users to cheaper payment options outside the App Store, and opening iOS features to third parties — do not originate in this ruling. They come from the DMA's gatekeeper rules themselves and from separate Commission enforcement, most notably the €500 million anti-steering decision of 23 April 2025, the first fine ever levied under the DMA, which found that Apple's restrictions prevented developers from directing users to cheaper options; Apple has appealed that fine, and it remains pending. There is one architecture that removes the third-party-disclosure problem, the retained-log problem, and the privacy-policy problem entirely: running an open-weight model locally, on hardware the firm owns and controls. “Local AI” (also called on-device or self-hosted AI) means downloading the weights of an open-weight large language model -- Meta’s Llama family, Mistral’s open models, DeepSeek’s V3/R1 releases, Alibaba’s Qwen family, OpenAI’s gpt-oss releases, and many others published on repositories such as Hugging Face-- and running inference entirely on a local machine using a runtime such as Ollama, LM Studio, or llama.cpp. The prompt never leaves the machine. No query is transmitted to any provider’s servers; no chat log is created on third-party infrastructure; no privacy policy or terms of use governs the interaction; and there is no provider to subpoena, no log to freeze under a litigat

title: Post by Risk Management
description:
# Post by Risk Management · LinkedIn · 2026-07-10

**Risk Management**: Founded in 2021.

---

Risk Management Excel Dashboard Suite: https://lnkd.in/d8qwAahr
AI governance is not a policy document.

It is a continuous risk management discipline.

The NIST AI RMF helps organizations structure that discipline through four connected functions:

✓ Govern accountability, ownership, and oversight
✓ Map context, stakeholders, impacts, and dependencies
✓ Measure safety, reliability, privacy, fairness, and performance
✓ Manage treatment actions, residual risk, incidents, and monitoring

For generative AI, this also means addressing prompt injection, data leakage, synthetic content misuse, model drift, supplier dependencies, and explainability.

The real test is not whether an organization has AI principles. It is whether every AI system can be inventoried, risk-tiered, tested, monitored, governed, and evidenced.

How mature is your organization’s AI risk management approach?

#NISTAIRMF #AIGov

description: UK HR teams are entering a new phase of data protection compliance after the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amended the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018, with many of the employment-related changes taking effect from 5 February 2026. The revised framework is intended to be more workable for organisations, but it also raises the bar on process, documentation and accountability, particularly where recruitment technology, employee complaints and subject access requests are concerned. The government says the reforms are designed to simplify rules while preserving strong privacy protections.\n...\nThe biggest operational shift may be in automated decision-making. Government guidance says the Act creates a more permissive regime, and the ICO has continued to stress that organisations using automated systems must still build in safeguards, including human review, a chance to challenge the outcome and transparency about how decisions are made. In HR, that matters most for recruitment fil

title: ‘Consult first’ duty considered for use of employee monitoring tech
description:
The option is one of three interventions the UK government is considering making in response to “the pace of advancement in workplace technologies” – including the use of AI. Alternative interventions could include the introduction of a statutory code, together with guidance, to “strengthen clarity and support more consistent good practice”, or publication of new non-statutory guidance. The consultation does not identify a preferred approach, and the purpose of the exercise is to gather evidence on whether intervention is required and, if so, what form it should take.
...
The paper highlights the range of requirements that employers already face in relation to their use of WMTs – including obligations under data protection law.

title: Europe's Top Privacy Regulators Just Confirmed AI Hiring Tools Have Been Breaking the Law for Eight Years - Founder News
description: The conference’s timing was deliberate. On June 29, 2026, the Council of the European Union gave final approval to the “Digital Omnibus” simplification package, formally pushing back the compliance deadline for stand-alone high-risk AI systems under Annex III of the EU AI Act from August 2, 2026, to December 2, 2027 — a 16-month reprieve. The EU AI Act classifies AI systems applyd in employment-related decisions as high-risk, including tools applyd for recruitment, candidate selection, performance evaluation, tinquire allocation, monitoring of workers, and decisions on promotion or termination.\n...\nThe EDPB has launched its Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, with 25 data protection authorities across Europe assessing whether controllers are meeting their transparency obligations under GDPR Articles 12, 13, and 14, which require individuals to be clearly informed when their personal data is being processed. title: License To Model: Emerging US Rules Impact Global Access To Frontier AI - New Technology - United States
description: On June 2, 2026, the White House issued an executive order titled Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security (Executive Order). The Executive Order established a voluntary framework through which developers of certain advanced AI systems may provide the federal government with access to models before public release for cybersecurity and national-security review. The framework contemplates government review of up to 30 days and focuses on advanced cyber capabilities and other national-security concerns. The Executive Order expressly states that it does not create mandatory licensing, pre-clearance or permitting requirements.4\n...\nThe administration has emphasized that the framework is voluntary and intended to balance continued US AI leadership with growing concerns regarding cybersecurity and critical-infrastructure protection. It should be published in the Official Journal shortly and enter into force three days later. The key message is that although some deadlines have changed, you still need to be planning for them.

title: EU AI Act Enforcement Timeline: A Deployer's On-Premises Readiness Checklist by Phase
description:
The EU AI Act does not switch on all at once. It applies in phases, and for most enterprises the phases that matter are still ahead. The prohibitions and AI-literacy provisions already apply. General-purpose AI model obligations have begun. The obligations that touch the largest number of organisations — those for high-risk AI systems — arrive in the 2026-2027 window, with a proposed amendment that may shift parts of that timeline.
...
This is the phase most enterprises are preparing for. Under the Act’s statutory timeline, the core obligations for Annex III high-risk systems apply from 2 August 2026, alongside the Article 50 transparency requirements and the start of national and EU-level enforcement.
...

title: Web scraping for AI training: EDPB draft guidelines
description: In short: Web scraping for AI training is lawful under the GDPR only where a data controller has a lawful basis for it, and in practice that means the three-part legitimate interest test in Article 6(1)(f). On 8 July 2026 the European Data Protection Board adopted draft guidelines on web scraping for generative AI, out for consultation until 30 October 2026. UK data controllers face a closely aligned but not identical position under UK GDPR and the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025.\n...\nA UK data controller cannot disregard the EDPB guidelines. Under Article 3(2) of the EU GDPR, a UK developer that offers its model to people in the EEA, or that monitors their behaviour, is caught by the EU regime for that processing regardless of where the scraping happens. For those businesses the guidelines are the operative standard, and the reach is not theoretical. It runs both ways: in Information Commissioner v Clearview AI Inc [2025] UKUT 319 (AAC), the Upper Tribunal held that the ICO had jurisdi

title: NCSC guidance on generative AI in plain English
description:
The NCSC sorts generative AI into three tiers. Public LLMs are open to anyone over the internet, and the do-not-input rule applies to them in full. Cloud-provided private LLMs are bought under a business contract, with clearer terms on how your data is handled. Self-hosted models run on your own infrastructure and are realistic only for large organisations. The same product often spans two tiers.

The sensible sequence is to experiment on public tools with nothing sensitive in play, then move any workflow that touches organisational data onto a contracted business plan, where you can check the data processing agreement, control

title: Global Standards for AI governance and business compliance
description: The global standards for AI governance that U.S. companies must follow are no longer merely voluntary suggestions. The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) entered into force on August 1, 2024, with fines of up to €35 million or 7% of annual worldwide turnover — exceeding the GDPR. Three frameworks now set the baseline: the EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001:2023, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. This guide explains what each requires, which applies to U.S. companies, and how to build a program that satisfies more than one at a time.\n...\nProhibitions on unacceptable-risk AI became enforceable on February 2, 2025. General-purpose AI model obligations took effect on August 2, 2025. Under the Digital Omnibus provisional agreement of May 7, 2026, most high-risk Annex III obligations are now deferred to December 2, 2027. However, the prohibited-practice bans and GPAI rules are already enforceable and unchanged. Companies that wait until December 2027 to start will miss the documentation and

title: NIST AI RMF coverage/analysis (risk‑based guidance that encourages mitigations including local/private processing)
description: NIST AI Risk Management Framework remains the leading US risk‑management guidance for AI (coverage and analysis in 2026); while it does not mandate on‑device inference, its risk‑based approach and emphasis on risk mitigation makes local/private processing (or stronger controls over cloud inference) a recognized organizational mitigation for high‑sensitivity datasets.

title: CNIL enforcement (large fines) — reinforces risk of third‑party data sharing
description: CNIL and other European DPAs have continued high‑value enforcement (examples in the last 12 months include a CNIL €3.5M cookie/tracking fine (30 Dec 2025) and very large privacy fines reported for ad/consent failures), demonstrating an enforcement climate that raises the financial risk of careless third‑party data sharing (i.e., sending employee or large subscriber behavioural datasets to external LLM/cloud providers).

title: UK ICO publishes updated guidance on storage and access technologies
description: UK Information Commissioner's Office updated guidance (storage and access technologies) published April 29, 2026 — guidance addresses storage/access choices and is part of the UK trend of urging cautious use of third‑party processing; this strengthens incentives for organisations to avoid sending sensitive employee/HR data to third‑party cloud LLMs without robust safeguards.

title: European Commission: Cloud and AI Development Act (policy to build secure interoperable EU cloud)
description: European Commission introduced the 'Cloud and AI Development Act' (policy announcement) aiming to give European businesses access to secure, sustainable and interoperable cloud infrastructure — a policy that strengthens on‑region/private cloud alternatives and can structurally advantage EU/private-cloud or local-first AI deployments over reliance on third‑country public clouds.

title: European Commission draft guidelines on high‑risk AI system classification (Annex III)
description: European Commission published draft guidelines (May 19, 2026) on high‑risk AI system classification (Annex III) — guidance that could expand or clarify which workplace/productivity systems are high‑risk (e.g., systems used for hiring, performance management), thereby increasing legal incentives for local/private processing or stricter controls on cloud LLM use in organisations.

title: EDPB finalises guidance on data transfers and AI training (strengthens transfer restrictions)
description: EDPB adopted final guidance tightening rules on transfers of personal data (including for AI training/processing) to third countries; guidance and training materials explicitly address AI and data protection, increasing pressure to keep sensitive/personal processing in-EU or under stronger contractual/private-cloud controls rather than handing data to third‑country cloud LLMs.

title: Belgian DPA Publishes Findings on AI Chatbot GDPR Compliance
description: In findings published in a July 2026 report, the Belgian Supervisory Authority (APD) highlighted that AI chatbot developers and operators prioritized development, user experience and scalability over GDPR compliance, citing issues with sensitive data in free-text interactions, data minimization and sharing with external providers.

title: US Supreme Court Ruling Questions FTC Independence in EU-US Data Privacy Framework
description: On 7 July 2026, a US Supreme Court ruling raised questions about the independence of the FTC, potentially undermining aspects of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework used for cross-border data transfers involving cloud AI processing.

title: EU Court Rejects Apple's Challenge Against DMA Rules
description: On 8 July 2026, the EU General Court rejected Apple's legal challenges against Digital Markets Act obligations, upholding rules on app store practices including data access and interoperability requirements that restrict certain data combination and cross-use by gatekeepers.

title: European Commission Presents EU Action Plan on Cybersecurity and Artificial Intelligence
description: On 7 July 2026, the European Commission presented the EU Action Plan on Cybersecurity and Artificial Intelligence, outlining a coordinated approach for Member States, businesses and public authorities to address cybersecurity and resilience challenges posed by advanced AI models.

title: EDPB Adopts Draft Guidelines on Web Scraping for Generative AI
description: On 8 July 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted draft Guidelines 03/2026 on web scraping in the context of generative AI for public consultation until 30 October 2026, clarifying GDPR legal bases, conditions for processing special categories of data, and mitigating measures including data minimization, exclusion of sensitive sources, prompt deletion or anonymization, and safeguards against model memorization.

title: Post by Paddle
description:
# Post by Paddle · LinkedIn · 2026-07-14

**Paddle**: Paddle is a merchant of record solution for B2B & B2C digital companies, helping increase global conversions, reduce churn, stay compliant, and scaling up fast. Paddle has 300-400 employees (+8-9% YoY) and has annual revenue in the GBP20M-GBP30M range. Headquartered in London, England, United Kingdom, operates in 20 countries (including Portugal, Canada, United States, Netherlands, and Spain). with 11 prior funding rounds.

---

In a world where anyone can build, some app founders are getting existential.

No, the "APPocalypse" isn’t here just yet. But AI is quietly reshaping how consumer apps get valued, priced, and acquired.

For most subscription businesses, the numbers are stronger than ever. Leaner teams, lower OPEX, better margins.

But underneath that, harder questions are being asked.

Volume-based models capture more spend but they make LTV murkier, cohorts harder to read, and acquirers more cautious about what comes next.

title: Post by AI Builders Hub
description:
# Post by AI Builders Hub · LinkedIn · 2026-07-03

**AI Builders Hub**: Founded in 2025.

---

New data shows who is really winning the consumer AI race.

It is not as simple as “ChatGPT is winning.”

And it is not as simple as “Claude is catching up.”

The real story is more interesting.

Sensor Tower’s State of AI 2026 report shows three apps dominating consumer AI:

ChatGPT.
Google Gemini.
Anthropic Claude.

ChatGPT still owns reach.

It has more than 1.1B monthly users, compared with Gemini at 662M and Claude at 245M.

So yes, ChatGPT is still the scale leader.

But scale is only one side of the race.

The more important signal for builders is this:

Claude is monetizing its smaller audience better.

According to reported Sensor Tower data, Claude’s US mobile ARPU reached $2.76 in May 2026, compared with ChatGPT at around $1.74.

Claude also leads major AI assistants on paid conversion, with around 13% of users paying for a subscription.

That changes the narrative.

ChatGPT built

Usage-based pricing means customers pay based on consumption, such as API calls, active seats that fluctuate, data processed, transactions run, or credits consumed, instead of a fixed subscription fee locked in at signup.
...
The pitch is obvious: pricing tracks value delivered, expansion happens automatically as customers grow, and you remove the friction of negotiating seat counts. Snowflake, Twilio, and Datadog built durable businesses on some version of this model, and OpenView Partners' research found that companies with usage-based pricing grow faster than those without it.

title: Hybrid Seat+Consumption Pricing for Mid‑Market SaaS | Usage Billing Report
description:
In 2025–26, an increasing number of mid‑market SaaS vendors adopted hybrid pricing that blends traditional seat or subscription licenses with consumption-based meters. That shift reflects sellers’ need to reconcile two competing priorities: predictable recurring revenue and a clear expansion path aligned to customer value. This article analyzes why mid‑market companies are embracing hybrid seat+consumption models now, the measurable tradeoffs for revenue and retention, and practical steps pricing teams should take before rolling out a hybrid program.
...
- Customer expectations: Mid‑market buyers increasingly want to pay for incremental value (e.g., data scanned, API calls, storage) while still keeping a base subscription for access, support and governance.
- Operational maturity: Billing systems, revenue recognition tooling and telemetry improved enough that product and finance teams can implement mixed meters at scale without crippling reconciliation work.

Anthropic is about to test whether consumers will pay cloud-style usage fees for a frontier chatbot. title: ChatGPT Workspace Agents Now Bill Credits on Top of Seats: The July 6 Cutover Math (2026) | UsageBox
description: TL;DR (July 2026): On July 6, 2026 the free preview of ChatGPT Workspace Agents ended. Every agent run inside a Business, Enterprise, or Edu workspace now draws down workspace credits on top of the per-seat fee you already pay. The published GPT-5.5 rates: 125 credits per million input tokens, 12.50 per million cached input tokens, 750 per million output tokens - a typical end-to-end run lands around 5 to 25 credits. What OpenAI did not publish is a consumption table for real workflows, so every team that automated prospecting, reporting, or ticket routing during the free spring now has a variable operating cost it cannot budget from the vendor's own docs. The seat is the floor; the meter is the bill. If you want a forecast, you have to build it from your own per-run usage data - nobody else has it.\n...\nThe July 6 cutover is the clearest single example of the pricing shift that has been rolling through AI software all year: the subscription seat stops being the price and becomes the en

Business Professionals and Consumers subscription revenue rose 16% year over year to $1.85 billion in Q2 FY2026, outpacing the 13% growth in Creative and Marketing Professionals subscriptions, which reached $4.54 billion. Acrobat AI Assistant's paid MAU growth of more than 150% suggests that everyday document work - not just high-end design - is becoming a real AI monetization surface. title: SaaS is Dead, Long Live SaaS Obligations | James Wang posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: SaaS is dead. Long live SaaS. Nobody was ever buying just code, which is why the obituary got filed early. AI writes software now, multiples compressed, and every application company got repriced as if a customer with an AI subscription is a customer about to churn. Code has been close to free at the margin for decades. You could always fork an open source project or hire a contractor. Companies mostly didn't, because the code was the least of what they were paying for. The contract buys a counterparty. Someone else carries the uptime, the security patches, the compliance certs, the integrations that break when a partner changes their API. Someone else answers the phone when the thing falls over during quarter close, which is when things fall over. You're renting an obligation, and the point is that the obligation belongs to someone who isn't you. AI changed the economics of exactly one item on that list. Producing code got cheap, and everything else stayed priced where it was, which m

title: Post by TTL Capital
description:
# Post by TTL Capital · LinkedIn · 2026-07-04

**TTL Capital**: TTL Capital has 1-10 employees, founded in 2025. Headquartered in Accra, Greater Accra Region, Ghana.

---

The benchmark on your board deck now fails 4 out of 5 public SaaS companies.
Rule of 40 — growth rate plus profit margin should clear 40 — became the default health check for software. In Q4 2025 the median public SaaS score was just 28%. Only about 20% of names cleared the bar (17 of 60).

Here's the part most founders skip: how you count "profit" decides the whole story. On an EBITDA basis, only 15% of public SaaS clear 40. On a free-cash-flow basis, 46% do. Same companies, same quarter — three times the "winners" depending on which line you pick.

Private is harder. Median score of 12%: roughly 10% growth, 6% margin. And the cause isn't overspending — margins have actually improved since 2022. Growth is what fell off.

So treat the Rule of 40 as a composition test, not a scoreboard. A 40 built on 35% growth and 5%

In this week’s Chart of the Week, Kingscrowd updated our equity crowdfunding revenue multiple benchmark for 2026, looking at companies that disclosed both valuation and at least $50K in annual revenue.

The good news: valuations become much more reasonable as companies grow. Startups generating more than $1M in annual revenue had a median revenue multiple of 8.4x.

The round was led by Aramco Ventures, the venture arm of the Saudi oil giant, with participation from Vista Equity Partners, General Catalyst, Emergence Capital, Nvidia, March Capital, Pegatron, and SentinelOne’s S Ventures. title: GitHub and Salesforce AI Pricing Experiments Yield $1.2B ARR | Peep Laja posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: GitHub and Salesforce are running AI pricing experiments for all of us to learn from. One of them repriced for the customer, the other repriced to survive. GitHub's version was desperate. In April they stopped accepting new paid signups entirely. Agentic coding blew up their compute costs, reportedly doubling weekly spend, and every new flat-rate customer made the math worse. They chose zero new revenue over two more months of that. June 1, they launched token billing for everyone. Devs posted projections of $29 a month becoming $750. TechCrunch's headline: "What a joke." Signups only started reopening mid-June. Salesforce ran its pricing pain in public instead. Agentforce launched at $2 per conversation. Adoption was tepid, implementation folks called it a non-starter. So they shipped credits at 10 cents an action. Then flat enterprise agreements, one negotiated fee, unlimited use. Their CRO's own words: 'we agree on a flat fee, and then it's a shared risk.' Three models in 18 months,

title: Exponential View Confirms the AI Supercycle: $175B Run Rate, 3× Faster Than Mobile - FourWeekMBA
description:
On June 25, 2026, Azeem Azhar’s Exponential View published the State of the AI Economy — the first publicly available, bottom-up, deduplicated measure of generative-AI commercial revenue. The headline figures: $110 billion in booked revenue over the trailing twelve months, and an annualized run rate now exceeding $175 billion. The ~1.6× gap between those two numbers is not rounding error — it is the encoded growth velocity of the market in real time.
...
The methodology matters as much as the numbers. The dataset covers app-layer, foundation-model, and infrastructure-hosting revenue across 1,000-plus companies. It is deduplicated — so API revenue that flows through an app is not double-counted at the model layer — and it is global ex-China. Critically, it excludes chip manufacturing: this is a software and services number, not an Nvidia hardware figure. When people cite the $175B, they need to carry that asterisk.

title: Opsgenie EOL: Upgrade to Modern On-Call Solution with Our Rescue Program | Chris Evans posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: I'm going to confess, I don't fully understand what Atlassian were thinking with Opsgenie. Back in June 2025 they emailed their customers to say that Opsgenie would reach end of life in 2027, and that they should move to either Compass or Jira Service Management — a move that opened the door for every customer to ask whether they're really working with the best product and team in the space. And so on April 5, 2027, it's gone for good, and you need to be migrated somewhere else. We built our Opsgenie Rescue Program to take the sting out of the process, making it easy to upgrade to an on-call product that's purpose built for modern engineering teams. We'll provide our services free of charge during the overlap with your existing contract, so you're never paying twice, import your existing setup rather than rebuilding it by hand, and back it all with up to 99.99% uptime. Having been through plenty of software migrations in my career, I know nobody loves them. This one may be unavoidable,

Based in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia [AU].

Most AI features ship, demo well, and never make a dollar.

After years shipping AI and platform products, the gap I see usually isn't model quality. It's that teams treat "we added AI" as the finish line — when it's actually the starting line for a monetization problem.

The features that turn into revenue tend to clear four gates, in order:

1. Job, not feature. The AI has to own a job the user was already paying for — in time or money — not add a novelty button. If you can't name the job, you're not selling anythi

title: Workday's AI Acquisitions: A Map of the Agent Platform | David Apollon posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: In under two years, Workday bought six AI companies. Most customers I work with have never seen the map in one place, so I drew it. 2024 was point solutions: HiredScore for talent intelligence, Evisort for contract intelligence. Folded into the record. Then the second half of 2025, over about fourteen weeks: Paradox for frontline hiring at roughly a billion, Flowise for building agents, Sana for the knowledge front door at about 1.1 billion, Pipedream for the wiring into every other system. That is not a shopping list. That is an agent platform assembled in one quarter. The through-line is a move from system of record to system of action. For twenty years Workday's moat was holding the authoritative record. The uncomfortable truth behind two billion in spending is that the record alone is no longer a moat, so Workday bought the front door and the ability to act. Four of the six have shipped. Two are still integration promises with dates. That difference matters when the roadmap slide b

title: Post by Melissa Rosenthal
description:
It came from the unglamorous stuff. Governed data models. A semantic layer that turns "weekly active users" into one definition instead of five conflicting ones. Encoded workflows. Validation systems that check the outputs.
...
This changes the whole enterprise knowledge conversation. For years, the pitch was "connect to everything, understand it, act on it," and that promise carried a premium. But if the real leverage lives in your own semantic definitions and a handful of encoded workflows, the buyer's question shifts.
...
Second, the jump from 21% to 95% is the cost, not the freebie. Building the governed datasets and the semantic layer is exactly the slow work most companies underfund. Skipping it is part of what these platforms actually sell.
...
So the takeaway isn't that AI replaces your data platform. It's closer to the opposite. The model layer is commoditizing. The connector layer is commoditizing. The defensible ground is the governed semantic layer underneath, and the trust

title: Post by Dr. Ashraf Elnashar
description:
For years, agents had amnesia — every session started from zero. Copilot Studio's Memory changes that: the agent keeps persistent, per-user context across interactions, learning a person's preferences and patterns over time.
...
1. Decide what Memory should store — and what it must not. Preferences and working patterns, yes. Sensitive personal details it doesn't need, no. Write the list down.
...
2. Set a retention period. Persistent doesn't mean forever. Decide how long a remembered preference lives before it expires — and set it before go-live, not after.
...
3. Wire the deletion path. Under GDPR and the EU AI Act, a person can ask to be forgotten. You need a working way to erase what Memory holds about them — tested, not theoretical.
...
4. Isolate strictly per user. One person's remembered context must never surface in another's session. Confirm the boundary holds.
...
5. Log what's stored. Treat Memory as a system of record — you should be able to answer "what does the agent know

# Apple WebKit · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

## Author

**Apple WebKit**

*Official LinkedIn account for the WebKit Open Source Project — the web browser engine that powers Safari and other apps on macOS, iOS, iPadOS, visionOS, Windows, and Linux.*

## Post

Agents are great at writing code, but they don't know how the browser renders it. The Safari MCP server changes that. Connect it to a live Safari window and your agent can check the DOM, read console output, inspect network requests, and take screenshots on its own. No more describing what you're seeing. It can check it out for itself. Available now in Safari Technology Preview 247. Works with Claude, Codex, and any MCP-compatible client.

title: Devin Desktop Persistent Memory (Formerly Windsurf)
description:
The hindsight-devin-desktop integration adds persistent long-term memory to Devin. It's worth understanding how it gets there, because Devin Desktop doesn't expose lifecycle hooks to third parties. There's no place to bolt a sessionStart recall or a stop retain. Instead the integration uses two things the editor does support: remote Model Context Protocol(MCP) servers and always-on workspace rules.
...
- Devin Desktop (formerly Windsurf) has no third-party lifecycle hooks, so memory is wired through MCP plus a rule, not hook scripts.
...
- hindsight-devin-desktop init connects the Hindsight remote MCP server (Devin gets recall / retain / reflect tools) and writes one always-on rule to .devin/rules/hindsight.md.
...
- No local daemon, no plugin scripts, no per-turn hooks. The MCP endpoint connects straight to Hindsight Cloud or your self-hosted server.

title: Claude gets permanent memory via open-source plugin, and 46K developers noticed in two days
description:
If you’ve ever watched an AI coding assistant forget everything you told it five minutes ago, you understand the specific flavor of frustration that drove Alex Newman to build claude-mem. The open-source plugin, which gives Anthropic’s Claude Code a persistent memory layer, racked up roughly 46,000 GitHub stars in about 48 hours after launching in early April 2026.
...
Claude Code, like most AI coding assistants, is fundamentally stateless. Every session starts fresh, with the model having zero recollection of your previous conversations, decisions, or debugging rabbit holes.
...
Claude-mem tackles this by capturing data from coding sessions, including tool calls and session events, then generating semantic summaries that get stored in a local SQLite database. When you start a new session, the plugin automatically injects relevant historical observations so Claude already has context about your project.
...
The installation is a single command: npx claude-mem install. No cloud accounts

title: Is There an Open Standard for AI Agent Memory Engrams? - DEV Community
description: The short answer: No single RFC-level standard exists for AI agent memory engrams as of mid-2026. The closest things are the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an open protocol from Anthropic that standardizes how applications expose context to LLMs — and the Engram Specification (Apache-2.0), an open format published by PLUR that defines the data structure for portable agent memory. Together they address the transport layer and the data layer, but neither has achieved IETF-level standardization. The space is still fragmenting: Mem0, Letta, Zep, Cognee, and a dozen other projects each define their own memory schemas, and no interoperability standard has merged them yet.\n...\nThe Engram Specification (plur.ai/spec.html), published in March 2026 under Apache-2.0 by the PLUR project, defines an open format for agent memory. An engram — a term borrowed from cognitive science, where it means the physical trace a memory leaves — is one atomic unit of learned knowledge: a single fact, stored as a

You can start interrogating Siri on your Mac, walk away, and pick it up mid-thought on your iPhone, iPad, Watch, or Vision Pro. It finally behaves like a thing you have a relationship with, rather than a vending machine you yell at.

title: Gemini Spark Lands on Mac: Local Files, MCP, and Real-Time Tracking Join Desktop AI Battle
description:
The Mac expansion puts Google in more direct competition with desktop AI agents that have already staked out local file access — including Anthropic's Claude Cowork, OpenAI's ChatGPT Codex, OpenClaw, and Microsoft Copilot — at a moment when the AI assistant market's center of gravity is shifting from the browser tab to the operating system.
...
The more significant structural addition is MCP support. The Model Context Protocol is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 and now stewarded by the Linux Foundation. It functions as a universal adapter: instead of Google hand-curating every integration, developers can build MCP servers for their own apps and plug them directly into Spark. Client and server communicate via JSON-RPC 2.0, with two transport options — standard input/output for local connections and server-sent events for remote streaming.
