title: Glaze by Raycast turns a chat into a real Mac app — and hit 1 on Product Hunt with 494 upvotes – Top AI Product
description:
Glaze by Raycast turns a chat into a real Mac app — and hit 1 on Product Hunt with 494 upvotes – Top AI Product

July 3, 2026

## Glaze by Raycast turns a chat into a real Mac app — and hit 1 on Product Hunt with 494 upvotes

Raycast, the team behind the keyboard launcher power users swear by, just shipped Glaze. You describe the app you want in plain language, and the AI builds a real desktop app — icon in your dock, launches instantly, no browser tab pretending to be software.

## What it actually is

Not a code generator that hands you a repo. Glaze produces a native Mac app that runs offline and reaches into your machine: local files, CLIs, keyboard shortcuts, menu bar, background processes. It can also hit any external API or pull in AI models, so a two-sentence prompt can become a working tool wired to the services you already use. Don’t like a button? Keep chatting, or point-and-annotate the exact element to change. Ship it to the public store or keep it private for your team.

The launch — first announced in March 2026 and rolled out via invitations in June — pushes the emerging category of AI-assisted app building out of the developer terminal and onto ordinary desktops. A free tier includes a one-time credit bundle sufficient to build one or two apps.

The app compiles and installs builds automatically, calls image-generation models natively, and lets users edit apps while they're running — including drawing a circle around a UI element to tell the coding agent what to change. title: Every modern Mac hides a free local AI, and I got mine answering questions in Home Assistant
description:
When you hear "Apple Intelligence," you probably think of features like the writing tools, the notification summaries, all of the other features that Apple markets under that name. However, underneath all of it sits an actual language model, running locally on the Neural Engine of every Apple Silicon Mac on macOS. It's sitting there, ready and waiting, and you can actually use it yourself if you know how.
...
One way to access it is through a small tool called Apfel, which takes that hidden model and exposes it as a normal AI server. It's the same kind of endpoint you'd point Ollama or an OpenAI client at, and once it's a server, anything that speaks that protocol can use it. Not only can you speak with it like any other model, but you can even do some crazy things, like link it up to Home Assistant. So, I connected it up, and with just my laptop, my smart home can now answer questions that most can't. And it's all running on my own hardware with no cloud or API bill.

title: New macOS malware steals passwords by posing as Apple's crash-reporting tool - Help Net Security

...

description:
Apple has released an update to XProtect for all macOS – The Eclectic Light Company

Apple has just released its first update to XProtect for all versions of macOS since early June, bringing it to version 5351; the previous version was 5347. As usual it doesn’t release information about what security issues this update might address.

This version adds six new Yara rules for MACOS.ANGRYORB.OX, MACOS.BOATLOAD.STCRST, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PRIN, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PEME, MACOS.VSHELL.DITA and MACOS.VSHELL.DR, amends rules for MACOS.MISOMAGIC.PA, and tidies spacing for those for MACOS.SOMA.STTX. From the rules for Boatload, that would appear to be a stealer.

Osascript rules in XPScripts.yr gain no less than 14 new rules, and amend those for MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TIPA, MACOS.OSASCRIPT.SYPR, MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TADE and MACOS.OSASCRIPT.REOB.

You can check whether this update has been installed by opening System Information via About This Mac, and selecting the Installations item under Software.

A full listing

# Post by Konstantin Klyagin · LinkedIn · 2026-07-05

**Konstantin Klyagin**: Founder at Redwerk with 31 years 5 months of experience. Previous roles include Senior Software Developer at Nokia, Developer at Open Source Community, and Periodical contributor and columnist at Software Wydawnictwo. Based in Lisbon Metropolitan Area [PT].

---

In March, a founder pinged me about his iOS app being stuck in App Store review for weeks. He had built it on Replit, vibe-coded the whole thing, quite proud of how fast it went from idea to live demo.

The demo genuinely looked good. Smooth, functional, the kind of thing that used to take months. He was ready to ship. Then Apple went quiet on him.

I dug into what actually happened. Turns out Apple didn't ban vibe coding. It enforced Guideline 2.5.2, a rule that has existed since the App Store launched: apps must be self-contained. They cannot download or execute code that changes their own functionality.

What Replit does is run your generated app

title: CrashStealer: New macOS Infostealer Uses Signed Apps to Evade Gatekeeper

Today, the Court of Justice of the European Union upheld in full a September 2022 General Court judgment which, in turn, had essentially upheld a July 2018 decision in which the European Commission established that, in order to strength and protect its general search service, Google had abused a dominant position by: (i) requiring manufacturers to pre-install the Google Search app and browser app (Chrome), as a condition for licensing Google's app store (the Play Store); and (ii) preventing manufacturers wishing to pre-install Google apps from selling even a single smart mobile device running on alternative versions of Android that were not approved by Google (Android forks). Congratulations to everyone involved on the European Commission side, including most recently Fernando Castillo de la Torre Carlos Urraca Caviedes, Brice Allibert, João Vareda, Massimiliano Kadar and Gunnar Wolf

title: Post by Ignacio Cofone
description:
The AI Act was built to catch high-risk systems before they reach the market, and the regulation the Council adopted on 29 June moves that obligation from August 2026 to December 2027 for stand-alone systems and to August 2028 for those built into products.
...
What using a "giving more time to comply" framing makes one miss is how postponement interacts with non-retroactivity under Article 111. A system placed on the market before the new date falls under the obligations only if it's substantially modified, so moving the date lets high-risk systems deployed before the new date run outside the obligations as long as they're not substantially changed. A system placed on the market before December 2027 but operating after it can sit outside the Act indefinitely unless altered after that date.
...
Tld;dr: Because the AI Act doesn't apply retroactively, the delay works as a standing exemption for everything deployed in the 2026-2028 window that it opens.

title: AI Regulation News | July, 2026 (STARTUP EDITION)
description:
Here is why this month matters. The European Union already has the European Union AI Act legal framework, which remains the clearest comprehensive AI law in force globally. At the same time, the United States still leans on a patchwork of federal agency actions and state laws, while the UK keeps pushing a principles-based path and other jurisdictions keep mixing privacy, platform, liability, and security rules into their AI approach. That creates opportunity, but also a compliance mess for small teams.
...
The strongest reference point remains the global overview of AI regulation developments and, more concretely, the EU AI Act. The Act uses a risk-based model. In plain English, that means regulators do not treat all AI systems the same. A toy image filter is not judged like an automated hiring system, biometric identification tool, or model used in health, education, public services, finance, or law enforcement.

title: A deep dive into the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026 | Insights | Matheson
description: The AI Act came into force in August 2024, and takes effect on a phased basis, with the majority of its provisions coming into force on 2 August 2026, subject to certain exceptions. In particular, the rules for high-risk AI systems are expected to be delayed until 2 December 2027 (for standalone high-risk AI systems under Article 6(2) and Annex III of the AI Act) and 2 August 2028 (for AI systems embedded as safety components and covered by EU sectoral legislation under Article 6(1) and Annex I of the AI Act) (as discussed here). The Bill largely reflects the approach set out in the General Scheme of the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Bill 2026 (the “General Scheme”) which was published in February 2026 (previously discussed here). The Irish Government has indicated its intention to adopt the Bill as soon as possible to ensure that the AI regulatory framework is fully operational in Ireland.\n...\nThe Bill provides MSAs with the power to seek access from providers of AI systems to

title: EU Just Delayed The AI Act's High-Risk Rules By 16 Months — Here's What Still Applies August 2, 2026 - LifeLogic Media
description:
On June 29, 2026, the Council of the European Union gave final approval to a package that pushes back the EU AI Act’s biggest compliance deadline by 16 months. If you’ve been bracing for August 2, 2026 as the date high-risk AI obligations kick in, that date just moved — but not all of it, and not for everyone. Here’s exactly what changed, what didn’t, and what to actually do about it.
...
- High-risk obligations are delayed, not cancelled: Annex III (use-based) high-risk systems now have until December 2, 2027 — not August 2, 2026. Annex I (product-regulated, e.g. medical devices, machinery) moves to August 2, 2028.
...
- August 2, 2026 still matters: Prohibited practices (already in force since February 2025), GPAI penalty powers, and national market surveillance enforcement authority all remain tied to that date.
...

title: EU AI Act vs. GDPR: How the Two Regulations Work Together | AITENCY
description: The EU AI Act and GDPR are complementary layers of European law, not alternatives: the AI Act regulates the AI system — how it is built, classified by risk, and monitored — while GDPR regulates the personal data flowing through it, and where an AI system processes personal data both apply at once. They overlap heavily on impact assessments, human oversight, transparency, risk management, and documentation, so a single well-built control can discharge duties under both. But the AI Act adds obligations GDPR never had — risk classification, conformity assessment, and post-market monitoring of the system itself — which means you can be fully GDPR-compliant and still breach the AI Act. Real tension exists too, most sharply between the AI Act demand for representative training data and GDPR data minimisation, and it must be resolved by governance rather than left to two separate teams. Both regimes bind the organisation deploying the AI, not just the vendor. The efficient answer is one unifi

title: EU AI Act: What Changes on 2 August 2026? | NetNordic
description:
On 2 August 2026, the EU AI Act enters a new phase — though not the one many organisations have been preparing for. Transparency obligations under Article 50 take effect, and the AI Office and national authorities gain full powers to enforce and sanction breaches, including for general-purpose AI (GPAI) models. What doesn’t happen on this date, following a legislative change adopted this summer, is the broad rollout of high-risk AI obligations that was originally scheduled.
...
- GPAI enforcement becomes operational — the substantive obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models (Articles 51–55) have applied since 2 August 2025. What changes now is that the AI Office can act on its enforcement and sanction powers over those providers, including investigations and administrative fines.
...
- High-risk AI systems (Annex III) are, notably, not part of this date anymore. The EU’s “Digital Omnibus,” which amends the AI Act, received final approval from the Council on 29 June 2026.

title: EU Anonymization Rules Tightened: AI Inference Attacks Drive New Three-Test Standard
description:
The European Union's top data protection authority adopted new rules Wednesday that formally close a compliance assumption tens of thousands of organizations have built their data practices around: that stripping identifying fields from a dataset makes it anonymous for GDPR purposes. The European Data Protection Board's Guidelines 02/2026 on Anonymisation replace the previous working definition with a three-test standard that regulators say reflects the real capabilities of modern AI — capabilities that have made the old field-stripping approach legally unreliable.
...
The EDPB's framework tests data against three cumulative criteria. Data must resist individual isolation — no specific person's record can be singled out. It must resist linkage — it cannot be connected to other datasets in ways that enable identification. And it must resist inference — probabilistic or AI-powered derivation of attributes about specific individuals from the data must be prevented.

title: Post by Jenn McCarron
description:
Everyone read Legora's switch to consumption-based model as a pricing update. I read it as the first legal AI vendor shifting its own compute bill to the customer.
...
For 3 years, the vendor ate that risk. You paid a flat number, you used the tool as much as you wanted and if your usage spiked, that was the vendor's problem to absorb.
...
Consumption pricing shifts that.
...
Consumption is a more accurate way to price software that thinks for itself. A quick lookup and an agent grinding across 10,000 contracts are not the same amount of work and charging one flat number for both was always a loss leader.
...
Last week I spent a call teaching a procurement team what a single AI search actually consumes. They had no idea because they've been sold unlimited everything, told the price on the page was the price and left to assume usage was somebody else's problem.

# CNBC’s Post

3,007,025 followers

5h

- Report this post

Apple is in talks with a small Silicon Valley company that says it can shrink powerful artificial intelligence models enough to run directly on an iPhone, the startup's CEO told CNBC. PrismML, a Khosla Ventures-backed spinout from the California Institute of Technology, publicly released compressed versions of Alibaba's open-source Qwen model on Tuesday. The company said it reduced the model from roughly 54 GB to less than 4 GB, allowing all 27 billion of its parameters to run on an iPhone 15 or newer. PrismML CEO Babak Hassibi told CNBC that Apple and other companies have been evaluating the startup's models and measuring their speed, energy efficiency and performance on devices.

title: Apple Extends Private Cloud Compute to Google Cloud, Redrawing the Trust Boundary for AI
description: Quietly but significantly, Apple has shifted one of the most important trust boundaries in consumer AI infrastructure. In a new expansion of Private Cloud Compute, Apple said it will run some Apple Intelligence workloads on Google Cloud, marking the first time PCC has operated beyond Apple-owned data centers, according to the Apple Security Blog and reporting from InfoQ. The move matters because PCC was created to avoid ordinary cloud trust assumptions and replace them with verifiable guarantees, as Apple explained in its earlier Private Cloud Compute architecture post. By transplanting that model onto third-party infrastructure, Apple is testing whether confidential AI can remain credible when the operator, hardware stack, and physical facilities are no longer fully under one vendor’s control.\n...\nApple’s move is strategically clever because it expands AI capacity without asking users to accept a lower-trust privacy model. Announced at WWDC 2026, the collaboration stacks three layers of hardware trust on top of each other: NVIDIA Confidential Computing on Blackwell GPUs, Intel TDX on the CPUs, and Google's Titan chip anchoring the root of trust.
...
PCC handles AI workloads too demanding for on-device models: agentic tool use, complex reasoning, and the next generation of Apple Foundation Models that Apple and Google built together using technologies behind Google's Gemini family. Until recently, every PCC request ran exclusively on Apple silicon in Apple-controlled data centers. The Google Cloud expansion means Apple's most privacy-sensitive cloud AI workloads will now execute on infrastructure that Apple does not own or operate.

description:
Second, expansion revenue got harder. NRR above 130% was common in 2021 because customers were growing, hiring, and rapidly adding seats. In 2026, customers are consolidating SaaS spend, eliminating tools that don’t drive clear ROI, and pushing back on auto-renewal expansion clauses. The companies that maintained NRR above 120% in 2026 are doing it through deliberate expansion motion — usage-based pricing, product-led upgrades, AI-feature monetization — not through passive seat expansion.
...
Third, the bar for what counts as capital-efficient changed. The 2021 mantra was
...
grow at all costs.” The 2026 mantra is “grow efficiently or don’t grow.” Magic number thresholds that were aspirational in 2021 are now minimum-viable, and the Rule of 40 — long considered a stretch goal — has become a sanity check that VCs apply to every new investment memo.
...
The Rule of 40 — a company’s growth rate plus its free cash flow margin should sum to at least 40% — has become the most-watched composi

title: Ken Kubec on LinkedIn
description: Bending Spoons went public this week. The stock priced at $29 and jumped roughly 38% on day one, pushing the company past a $25 billion valuation. Most of the LinkedIn feed I've seen read it as another software IPO. I think that framing misses the point entirely. Bending Spoons is not just a software company. It is arguably the first publicly traded, AI-native software holding company built to acquire neglected digital assets and compound them, the way Constellation Software Inc. compounds vertical-market software or Berkshire Hathaway compounds insurance float. The difference is what AI does to the underlying math. Start with the numbers, because they are hard to argue with: *2025 Revenue of $1.31B(+95% yoy) *84% 3yr Revenue CAGR *Adj Operating Income ~ $613M, (47% margin) *Q1 2026 Revenue $601M (+130% yoy%) *Adj Operating margin pushed past 50% *The company swung from a $112M net loss a year earlier to a $27.5M net profit Now the metric that actually explains the model: Revenue per e

title: Cybersecurity Vendors See 14.2% Revenue Growth in Q1 2026 | Matthew Ball posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: Boards are now scheduling security reviews around AI model releases, not incidents. That shift showed up clearly in Omdia's Q1 2026 Cybersecurity Titans Index. Palo Alto Networks fielded over 1,200 customer strategy review requests after the latest frontier model launches. Tenable held hundreds of advisory calls. CrowdStrike launched Project Quiltworks. The trigger in each case was a model release, not a breach. Each major release now compresses sales cycles, and we expect this to be the single biggest demand catalyst in the second half. The numbers back it up. The 20 largest listed cybersecurity vendors grew revenue 14.2% year over year to $15.2bn, beating the top of consensus. Fifteen beat their own high-end guidance. Four structural shifts stood out: • AI security is now a discrete budget category. It sits alongside endpoint, network, and cloud spend, not inside it. Seven Titans spent nearly $2bn on acquisitions in twelve months. Crowdstrike's AIDR grew ARR over 250% sequentially. P

title: AI Maturity Boosts PE Revenue 31x | McKinsey & Company posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: The biggest AI opportunity in private equity may go far beyond productivity. PE-backed companies with the highest levels of AI maturity traded at a median revenue multiple of 31x between 2023 and 2025, compared with 13x for companies taking a more opportunistic approach. The takeaway? The greatest value may come from using AI not only to improve operations, but to rethink products, business models, and future growth. https://mck.co/4oZzsPC\n...\nMcKinsey & Company’s latest research outlines the four distinct levels of AI value creation, highlighting the definitive dividing line between chasing basic productivity and true operating-model transformation. The data sends an unmistakable message to enterprise leaders and investors alike—companies operating primarily at Level 1 (characterized by opportunistic, isolated administrative automation) see negligible improvements in their market multiples. In sharp contrast, organizations that advance up the maturity curve to Level 2—broadly embeddin

title: SaaS Revenue Multiples June 2026: Trends and Insights\n\nWhat’s the median revenue multiple for publicly traded SaaS compa
description: SaaS Revenue Multiples June 2026: Trends and Insights What’s the median revenue multiple for publicly traded SaaS companies, how has it evolved, and why? As of June 30th, 2026, the median revenue multiple was 3,93x, which is: 👉 35% lower YoY (6,06x at the end of June 2025), but rebounded 20% from the record-level low of 3,27x in the middle of April 2026 👉 79% lower from all-time high (19.04x on Dec 18, 2020) 💡 Why the drop since 2020? It’s all about slower growth. The median YoY revenue growth rate for SaaS companies is now 20%, near the lowest levels since 2014 and far from the +30% YoY long-term growth in the last two decades until the end of 2022, which fueled higher multiples in previous years. ⚖️ Key takeaway: Slower growth = lower multiples. So, unless revenue growth will significantly increase, there is no coming back to valuations (revenue multiples) seen during the Tech Bubble (H2-2020 to H1 2022). But in the last months, the key driver that accelerated SaaS sell-off are most

# Post by TiDB, powered by PingCAP · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

**TiDB, powered by PingCAP**: TiDB is a distributed SQL database built for AI agents. They provide vectors, transactions, and analytics in one engine with ACID guarantees and elastic scale. TiDB, powered by PingCAP has 300-400 employees (+10% YoY) and has annual revenue in the $1M-$10M range, founded in 2015. Headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, United States, operates in 21 countries (including China, Singapore, Japan, India, and Malaysia). Has $341.6M in total funding, with 5 prior funding rounds.

---

Atlassian's Forge platform uses a one-schema-per-tenant model. Every customer gets their own isolated schema. At Atlassian's scale, that means over 3 million tables - all inside a single TiDB cluster.

The engineering write-up covers what it took to get there: the metadata bottlenecks, the TiDB internals that had to evolve, and the performance results on the other side.

Full breakdown 👉 https://lnkd.in/giH3WNYk

At Dropbox, our approach to and an async culture wasn't necessarily designed with AI in mind, but it encouraged us to build better habits around documenting work, sharing knowledge, and creating accessible context. The reality is that many of the biggest AI transformation challenges are actually operational ones. They come down to the

title: Zuckerberg: AI Agents Slower Than Expected — Meta 2026 | explainx.ai Blog | explainx.ai
description:
Mark Zuckerberg told Meta employees something the industry's marketing has spent two years denying: AI agents aren't progressing on schedule. At an internal town hall on July 2, 2026, first reported by Reuters, he said the "trajectory of the agentic development over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected," and that the company's bets on its restructured organization "haven't come to fruition yet."
...
The admission lands hard because of what preceded it. In May, Meta laid off about 10% of its global workforce and reassigned roughly 7,000 employees to AI-focused teams — moves premised on the belief that agents would absorb the work. Zuckerberg said executives planning the reorg in January and February were "super optimistic about tools like Claude Code." Meta is projected to spend up to $145 billion on AI infrastructure this year, part of Big Tech's $700 billion-plus outlay — spending we've tracked from the capex side and the bubble side.
...

title: Meta AI Agents Are Stalling: What Zuckerberg Admitted — Web Pulse
description: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta employees on Thursday that the company’s Meta AI agents development has not “accelerated in the way we expected” over the last four months — a candid admission that the $145 billion capital commitment and a May restructuring that eliminated 10,000 jobs are not yet delivering results. Reuters obtained a recording of the July 2 internal town hall. Zuckerberg also acknowledged the reorganization “wasn’t as clean as it could have been” and that executives had “miscalculated” on timing.\n...\nThis wasn’t a cautious experiment. In May 2026, Meta laid off roughly 10% of its global workforce and reassigned 7,000 employees to AI-focused teams — betting the company’s near-term velocity on AI agents replacing significant portions of its engineering and research workflows. Alexandr Wang, former CEO of Scale AI, was brought in to lead the newly formed Meta Superintelligence Labs. Meta raised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance to $125–145 billion, up from $115–135 billion,

Pilots succeed on paper, then stall in practice with a 95% pilot failure rate cited from MIT Nanda report. Multiple studies show implementation gaps; clinicians tune out tools that fail to deliver measurable outcomes.

title: The Hopium Around The AI Narrative Is Fading

Hyperscalers and tech giants like META are reconsidering their AI capex strategies, with some renting out excess data center capacity amid ROI doubts.

description:
The EU is opening funding instruments for Ukrainian companies working in dual-use technologies for the first time. At the Ukraine Recovery Conference (URC) in Gdańsk, the European Commission announced the launch of two new mechanisms under the Ukraine Facility. €340 million in guarantees and grants are expected to help mobilize up to €700 million in investment for the dual-use sector. Combined with already existing programs, the total support package reaches €500 million and has the potential to unlock up to €1.1 billion in financing.
...

For many HR teams, creating a new journey is a slow, inconsistent, and manual process. The more manual journeys are, the more likely it is that tasks get delayed, duplicated, or dropped, creating risk, frustration, and an awful lot of unnecessary work for HR. ROSI Journey Builder changes all of that. Here's how it works: 1️⃣ Describe your journey in simple language or upload a spreadsheet. 2️⃣ ROSI generates a complete journey in minutes. 3️⃣ Edit as needed before launching—without IT or professional services. ROSI Journey Builder from Rival is changing the game for HR. 🏃♀️➡️ Move quickly. ✅ Stay compliant. ⚖️ Deliver seamless experiences

title: Adam Boynton on LinkedIn
description:
# Adam Boynton · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

## Author

**Adam Boynton**

*Senior Enterprise Strategy Manager, Jamf | Making Apple & mobile a secure, governed enterprise platform | I build the business case enterprises sign | Keynote speaker*

Current role: Senior Enterprise Strategy Manager, EMEIA at Jamf

## Post

A lot of the AI running on your Mac fleet right now got there without anyone approving it. That is not a policy failure. It is just what happened. Your best people found the tools, Claude Code, Codex, the desktop assistants, and started using them at a scale and level of system access that governance has not caught up to. That is the reality underneath everything Jamf has shared this week. Plenty of excellent explanation and analysis from our leadership over the past few days, Katie English and Akash Kamath's videos especially. I wanted to do something a bit different and show it working on Mac. Around ninety seconds: see what AI is running across the fleet, configure how it beh

description: Workato®, the leading Control and Execution Platform for Enterprise AI, today announced two new capabilities for Agent Studio: Headless API and Agent Guardrails. Headless API lets Genies, Workato's AI agents built on Agent Studio, be embedded into any business application surface, on web, mobile, or inside another agent's own environment. Agent Guardrails are configurable to the business and ensure that wherever a Genie is embedded, it enforces company data privacy policies, ties every action to a real identity, and remains secure, auditable, and compliant by default.\n...\nHeadless API means Genies are no longer tied to a single interface. Teams can embed Workato's agentic AI directly into the products and tools employees already use, instead of building a new interface just to reach a Genie. title: Fable 5 redeployed with updated cybersecurity safeguards | Anthropic posted on the topic | LinkedIn
description: with the US government, we’ve updated our cybersecurity safeguards. The vast majority of coding work is unaffected. In the near term, the new safeguards will flag a slightly higher fraction of harmless requests than the previous Fable safeguards; we’re working to refine these over the coming weeks. Users will be clearly notified when a request is flagged, and they’ll instead receive a response from Opus 4.8. Our biology and chemistry classifiers are unchanged from our initial launch. These are still broader than we would like; they trigger fallbacks to Opus 4.8 on basic biology-adjacent questions. Improvements to these classifiers are landing soon. All paid plans with usage included can access Fable 5 through July 7. You can use Fable 5 up to 50% of your weekly usage limit, after which you can switch to another model for the remainder of your usage. You can also continue using Fable via usage credits. title: OpenAI rolls out GPT-5.6 after government greenlight — and announces ‘ChatGPT Work’ | The Verge
description:
About two weeks after OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 was caught up in regulatory drama— rolled out only to government-approved organizations during a “limited preview” period — the company has received the Trump administration’s greenlight for a public rollout of the model. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman called it“the best model we have ever produced.”
...
To celebrate, OpenAI also unveiled a new AI agent on the same day: ChatGPT Work. It’s billed as a combination of ChatGPT and Codex, allowing the everyday non-technical user to take advantage of Codex’s capabilities for non-coding tasks, and it’s powered by the GPT-5.6 model suite (Sol, Terra, and Luna). “It can gather context from the apps, files, and workflows you choose and create finished materials such as documents, spreadsheets, presentations, and web apps,” OpenAI wrote in a blog post, adding that a “unified plugins directory” allows ChatGPT to connect to tools like Slack, Gmail, Google Drive, calendars, and CRMs.

title: How to Control GPT-5.6 in Microsoft 365 Copilot Before the July 24 Mandatory Enablement - Windows News
description: Microsoft is rolling out GPT-5.6 as the preferred model in Microsoft 365 Copilot, but the move also makes OpenAI a subprocessor, shifting data processing flows. A new admin setting controls access, and unless explicitly blocked, GPT-5.6 will be enabled for all eligible commercial users on July 24, 2026. This analysis explains what the change means for end users and IT teams, the compliance gaps to check, and how to configure the setting before the deadline.\n...\nThe administrative change is more concrete. On June 23, 2026, Microsoft added OpenAI to its Online Services subprocessor list. A new tenant setting now controls access to “OpenAI-operated models.” Initially disabled, this setting will flip to “enabled for eligible users” on July 24 unless an AI Administrator or Global Administrator has set it to “No users.” The control lives in the Microsoft 365 admin center under Copilot settings for AI providers operating as Microsoft subprocessors. One thing he highlighted that landed with the room was agentgateway, which had just joined the AAIF as its fourth hosted project five days before the summit. Agentgateway is an open source gateway built specifically for agentic AI traffic: it unifies MCP connections, agent-to-agent communication, LLM inference, and REST/gRPC services under one control plane. Contributors include AWS, Microsoft, Cisco, Adobe, and Apple. The point Nalley was making is that the governance question and the infrastructure question are now being answered together. The protocol has a neutral home in the Linux Foundation, and the operational tooling needed to run it in production is following.

title: Why Coding Agents Forget, and What a Real Memory Layer Must Do — Hamed Taheri
description:
Start with the mechanics, because the marketing word "memory" hides them. Each Claude Code session begins with a fresh context window. Two disk-backed mechanisms carry anything across sessions, and both are re-loaded at launch. CLAUDE.md files are loaded in full at the start of every session, walked up the directory tree and concatenated. Auto memory (a`MEMORY.md` the agent writes for itself) loads only its first 200 lines, or 25KB, whichever comes first; topic files load on demand and stay machine-local, not shared across machines or teammates.

title: The AI Context War: Why Siri, Claude Tag, and Codex Are Solving the Same Problem | MindStudio
description:
Apple’s architectural choice here is significant: most of this processing happens on-device, using smaller models that run locally. This sidesteps the privacy concerns that would come with sending personal data to a cloud server, but it also means the models doing this work are constrained in size and capability.
...
The broader strategy is to pair on-device personal context with cloud-based reasoning for harder tasks. When Siri needs to do something more complex, it can pass the request (and relevant context) to Apple’s Private Cloud Compute infrastructure, or in some cases to external models like ChatGPT via the OpenAI integration Apple announced.
...
The Model Context Protocol (MCP), announced in late 2024, is essentially a standardized way for AI models to request information from external systems. A developer can build an MCP server that exposes a data source — a database, a file system, a SaaS tool — and any AI that speaks MCP can query it.

🚀 **llama.cpp b9888 just dropped — and it’s a quiet signal that the local AI race is getting sharper.**

Most people will read the changelog and yawn: “CUDA: extend K-type validation to V-types for flash attention” + some reorder + ARM64 binaries.

But here’s what actually changed for **production builders**:

**1. Flash attention just got more reliable.**
The validation fix isn’t about speed — it’s about **correctness under load**. If you’re running agents that chain multiple inference calls, subtle flash attention bugs can silently degrade outputs. This patch means fewer hallucinations and more predictable latencies on NVIDIA hardware.
→ *Frontier-agent builders benefit: your LLM ops are one step closer to deterministic.*

**2. The “reorder” matters more than it sounds.**

title: Your Mac's Next 'Crash Report' Might Actually Be Malware | Lifehacker
description:
If you're a Mac user, beware of any system prompts that request your credentials in order to send reports to Apple. A new malware—dubbed CrashStealer—is posing as a legitimate crash-reporting tool while stealing keychain data, local files, and data from browsers, password managers, and crypto wallets.
...
This macOS infostealer, which was identified by security firm Jamf, looks like a legitimate application. It goes by "CrashReporter.app," uses a recognizable icon and metadata, and is delivered by a dropper signed and notarized by Apple. Targets can download the installer from a fake software site promoting the "Werkbit" meeting platform and requires a PIN to access. From there, installation follows a similar process to any other real app, as the campaign relies on social engineering to trick targets into adding the payload directly to their own devices. As BleepingComputer describes, its technical setup allows it to avoid detection from macOS' anti-malware tool.
...
When the

title: macOS clipboard app Maccy has a fake out there stealing passwords - Digital Trends
description:
A fake version of Maccy, a popular clipboard manager for macOS, is being used to deliver a newly discovered Mac malware strain called PamStealer. Researchers at Jamf say the malware impersonates the real open-source app, but its actual purpose is to steal data and capture a victim’s login password.
...
PamStealer arrives as a disk image containing an AppleScript file that impersonates Maccy. Once the user opens that file, macOS launches it in Script Editor, where the on-screen instructions tell them to press Command-R. To someone expecting a normal app installer, that may look like an odd setup step. In reality, that action runs hidden malware code and starts the attack.
...
The first part of the attack is designed to stay quiet. Instead of using common Mac command-line tools that security teams often watch for, the researchers say the malware uses Apple’s own automation features to download and launch the next stage.
...
The payload then hides inside app bundles that pretend to be rea

description:

Uninstalling apps on macOS is usually very easy. You drag an app to the Trash, empty it, and move on. The annoying part is that many apps still leave residue behind, including support files, caches, preferences, containers, and logs. I have always found that frustrating, especially when old app data keeps sitting around long after the app itself is gone.

AppCleaner by FreeMacSoft has been the popular go-to option for this for years, and it still does the job well. But I recently came across a new open-source alternative called Uninstally by Codenta, which solves the same basic problem. It removes Mac apps along with the support files, caches, preferences, containers, logs, and other leftovers they usually leave behind.

title: Apple Services: Definitive Analysis & Regulation Risks
description: The evidence reveals a company methodically reengineering its business model around a triad of platform control, subscription lock-in, and privacy-as-a-service. The strategic acumen lies in leveraging regulatory and competitive pressures to justify new monetization vectors: AI features become differential subscription perks, while privacy infrastructure like PCC serves as both a developer incentive and a user trust moat. This dual-use approach transforms potential liabilities—antitrust mandates for interoperability, data localization demands, fee caps—into accelerants for an ever-expanding services portfolio. For market participants and enforcers, the critical tension lies between undeniable financial momentum (record services revenue, explosive developer billings, soaring AI-adjacent adoption) and the accumulating regulatory overhang that threatens the 30% commission structure, iCloud tying, and the Google payment stream. title: How Apple Makes Billions From the App Store—and Why 90% of Sales Now Escape Its Commission Cut | IBTimes UK
description:
Apple told developers on 4 June 2026 that its App Store moved more than £1T ($1.4T) in billings and sales during 2025, up from £974B ($1.3T) a year earlier, according to a study by economists at Analysis Group commissioned by the company. For more than 90% of that, developers handed Apple nothing. The slice it does tax lands almost entirely on the streaming and app subscriptions millions pay for monthly.

That means indie developers and small teams can offload to Apple’s cloud models at zero cost, a direct competitive move against API pricing from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google.

mobile app runs

Both Apple and Google run the same headline structure: a standard commission of 30 percent, and a reduced commission of 15 percent for smaller developers and for long-lived subscriptions. The entire game is understanding which rate applies to you.
...
On the Apple App Store, the standard commission is 30 percent of the price on most paid apps and in-app purchases. You drop to 15 percent in two situations. First, if you enroll in the App Store Small Business Program and earned under 1 million dollars in proceeds in the prior calendar year, everything moves to 15 percent. Second, auto-renewing subscriptions fall to 15 percent after a subscriber has been paying continuously for more than one year.
...

title: Council clears the AI Act rewrite as the enforcement clock keeps ticking
description: The timing is the story. The delay gives organizations more time for high-risk conformity work, but the AI Act’s broader Aug. 2, 2026, application date remains on track, including the AI Office’s full enforcement role. That leaves compliance pressure in place for obligations the omnibus did not substantially move, including the existing prohibited-practices regime, Article 50 transparency duties, and general-purpose AI oversight.\n...\nFor cybersecurity, data privacy, regulatory compliance, and eDiscovery professionals, the practical task is now classification and sequencing. Organizations should map AI systems against Annex III and Annex I, review their exposure to the new prohibitions on nudification and AI-generated child sexual abuse material, and prepare documentation, logging, retention, human oversight, and vendor governance processes for the parts of the Act that arrive first. title: 32 Days to the EU AI Act's Real Deadline — And What Brussels Quietly Deferred
description: Three enforcement mechanisms activate on the same day, and none of them were deferred. August 2, 2026 remains the date the Commission gains penalty enforcement powers over GPAI providers, the date Article 50 transparency obligations activate for most AI systems, and the date national market surveillance authorities can fully investigate and sanction AI Act breaches.\n...\nHere is the part that has received far less attention than it deserves. The Act arriving on August 2 is materially lighter than the one originally legislated, because of a deal reached this spring. The EU AI Act's enforcement timeline was substantially rewritten on May 7, 2026, when the EU institutions reached a provisional agreement on the Digital Omnibus on AI, deferring high-risk AI system obligations under Annex III to December 2, 2027 — sixteen months later than originally scheduled.This is a consequential softening. title: The $300B SaaS Collapse: Why Seat-Based Pricing is Dead
description:
For 20 years, the math of SaaS was simple: Headcount x Monthly Fee = Recurring Revenue. This model worked because headcount growth was traditionally tied to business expansion. If a company grew, they hired more people, and they bought more software licenses. Wall Street loved this predictability.
...
- Seat Displacement: A single agent handling invoice reconciliation can process the workload of five human employees.
- Revenue Collapse: If a customer replaces five human seats with one agentic system, the software vendor loses 80% of their revenue from that client.
- Inverted Growth: When this happens across an entire industry, the growth curves that sustained high SaaS valuations begin to invert.
...

The advanced software sector faced severe headwinds in 2026, with public equities underperforming the broader market and software leveraged loans falling to new lows. Fueled by SaaS-pocalypse panic and private equity funds enforcing gate redemptions against surging withdrawal requests, the market is heavily discounting software valuations.
...
However, this downturn masks the beginning of a historic AI margin super-cycle. The advanced software vertical is rapidly shifting from legacy seat-based licenses to outcome-based digital labor. With new agentic AI capabilities scaling human judgment into moat-protecting corporate IP, we expect this transition to be short-lived.
